stories.headline: HOUSE VOTES TO END PRESCRIPTION CURB; Liquor Liberalization Bill Is Passed 153-59 -- Roosevelt's Signature Is Due Today. FIGHT BY BLANTON FUTILE Texan Says Hard Drink Will Flow Freely -- Program for Modification Completed. HOUSE VOTES TO END PRESCRIPTION CURB
stories.summary: The US House of Representatives voted to end restrictions on liquor prescriptions, with President Franklin D. Roosevelt expected to sign the bill, as part of a broader effort to liberalize liquor laws during the Great Depression. This move is seen as a significant shift in the country's approach to liquor regulation.

stories.headline: PATRONAGE DELAY STRENGTHENS M'KEE IN WAR ON TAMMANY; Flynn Control Here Is Likely to Weaken Curry in Fight in Primary for O'Brien. ROOSEVELT STRATEGY AIDS Delay on Jobs Till End of Extra Session to Speed His Program Also a Help in City Battle. SMITH UNCERTAIN FACTOR Only a Decision by Him to Run for Mayor Would Block McKee -- Now Definitely Against It. FEDERAL JOBS AID M'KEE FIGHT HERE
stories.summary: President Franklin D. Roosevelt's strategy is aiding John V. McKee's fight against Tammany Hall, as delays in federal job appointments are expected to weaken the opposing faction, with Roosevelt to be consulted on federal appointments for New York. This power struggle is unfolding in the context of New York City politics.

stories.headline: EFFECTIVE THIS MORNING; Governor Acts After an All-Night Conference With Banking Heads. PERIOD INCLUDES MONDAY All Banks and the Securities Markets Are Affected by Emergency Decree. ILLINOIS TAKES STEP, TOO Period of Closing There Is Put at Three Days -- Pederal Officials in Conferences. 2-DAY BANK HOLIDAY IS LEHMAN'S ORDER
stories.summary: A governor invoked an emergency decree, resulting in a two-day bank holiday, closing all banks and securities markets, as part of a broader effort to address the banking crisis. This move is seen as a drastic measure to stabilize the financial system during a time of economic turmoil.

stories.headline: HITLER INTENSIFIES DRIVE ON THE LEFT; HUNDREDS ARRESTED; Red Leaders Jailed, Socialists' Papers Banned and Homes Searched All Over Prussia. NATION TOLD OF 'REVOLT' Goering Asserts Communists Planned to Trick Nazis Into 'Occupying' Berlin. SITUATION ALARMS LONDON Foreign Secretary Is Asked What Guarantee Britain Has of the Safety of Her Nationals. HITLER INTENSIFIES DRIVE ON THE LEFT
stories.summary: Adolf Hitler intensified his crackdown on the left, with hundreds of communists and socialists arrested, and their papers banned, as part of a broader effort to consolidate power in Germany. This move has alarmed foreign governments, including Britain, which is concerned about the safety of its nationals.

rounds.blurb: The US House of Representatives voted to end restrictions on liquor prescriptions. President Franklin D. Roosevelt was expected to sign the bill, liberalizing liquor laws. A two-day bank holiday was also invoked to stabilize the financial system.

stories.headline: EAST AND WEST.; Completion of the Great Line Spanning the Continent The Closing Work and Ceremonies at Promontory Summit. The News Flashed by Telegraph Simultaneously Over the Country. Rejoinings of the Metropolis at the Completion of the Enterprise. Celebrations in Chicago, Philadelphia and Other Cities. The Work Accomplished-Ceremonics at Promontory Summit. Announcement in Washington of the Completion of the Road-Scene in the Telegraph Office. Rejoicing in This City--The Booming of Cannon and Chiming of Bells--Magnificent Thanksgiving Services at Old Trinity--Address by Rev. Dr. Vinton--Congratulatory Dispatches to San Francisco by Mayor Hall and the Chamber of Commerce. The Southern Pacific--Meeting of Directors at St. Louis. Celebrations in Chicago, Philadelphia, Buffalo and Other Places. At the City Hall.
stories.summary: The transcontinental railroad was completed with ceremonies at Promontory Summit, and the news was celebrated across the country, including in Chicago, Philadelphia, and New York, during a time of major infrastructure development in the United States. The completion of the railroad was a significant achievement in connecting the east and west coasts.

stories.headline: DECORATION DAY; Floral Tributes to the Memory of the Union Dead Ceremonies at the National Cemetery at Washington Oration of General Butler at Gloucester, Mass Observances at Arlington--Presence of the President and Heads of Departments--Ceremonies at the "Tomb of the Unknown"--Oration of Hon. S. S. Fisher The Ceremonies at Gloucester--Oration of General B. F. Butler--His Eulogy of the Soldiers--Reference to Political Questions--The Alabama Claims Question--Furious Attack on England At Boston and Vicinity In Providence, R. C.--Parade of Veterans--Tolling of the City Bells In Philadelphia--Gathering in Independence Square--Procession of Children--Services in Concert Hall In Carlisle, Penn At Huntingdon, Penn.--Children's Observances At Knoxville, Tenn.-- Five Thousand Persons in Attendance--The Addresses At Richmond, Va At Cincinnati In Toledo, Ohio In Columbus, Ohio At Detroit, Mich In Newark, N. J. --Observances at Fairmount Cemetery At Flushing-Decorations of the Mounment to the Flushing Heroes
stories.summary: Decoration Day ceremonies were held at the National Cemetery in Washington, with President Ulysses S. Grant and other government officials in attendance, to honor the Union dead, and similar observances took place in other cities, including Boston and Philadelphia. General B. F. Butler delivered an oration at Gloucester, Massachusetts, as part of the ceremonies.

stories.headline: EUROPE.; Letters from Our Correspondents in London, Madrid and Florence Our Relations with England---Effects of Mr. Sumner's Speech The Warwickshire Scandal--- Sad Revelations Important and Interesting Debates in the Spanish Cortes The subjoined letters from our European correspondents present an interesting summary of political, social and Industrial affairs in the old world The Debate on the Irish Church Bill-Summary of the Views of Leading Statesmen on the Question-Sharp Debate on the Maynooth Endowment clause Labors of the Ministry--The Case of the Mayor of Cork--The Pacific Railroad The Debates on the Constitution--Another Great Orator Developed--Thrilling Souvenirs of the Inquisition--The Separation of Church and State--Impotent Fury of the Clergy Parties in the Chamber--The New Fusion--Proposed Reconstruction of the Ministry--The Sub-Alpine Tunnel and the Suez Canal--the Alabama Question--King Victor Emmanuel Assurances of an American Citizen to the English People--Letter From Senator Grimes, of lowa
stories.summary: Letters from European correspondents discussed various topics, including the effects of Mr. Sumner's speech on relations with England and debates in the Spanish Cortes, providing insight into international affairs during this period. The letters also touched on the Irish Church Bill and the separation of church and state.

stories.headline: Our Trouble with England--Mr. Summer's Speech--The Administration.
stories.summary: The Alabama Claims question and Mr. Sumner's speech were causing tension in relations with England, with the Administration facing challenges in addressing the issue, during a time of complex international diplomacy. Senator Sumner's speech had significant implications for the United States' relationship with England.

rounds.blurb: The transcontinental railroad was completed with ceremonies at Promontory Summit. The news was celebrated across the country, marking a significant achievement in connecting the east and west coasts. Decoration Day ceremonies also honored the Union dead.

stories.headline: BERTAUD MAY LOSE POST IN BELLANCA; ITS FLIGHT IN DOUBT; Levine Considers Ousting Him, Insists Hop Will Be Made With Chamberlin as Pilot. BYRD'S AIDE DECLINES JOB Bertaud Wants to Buy Plane--Thomas L. Hill Also Offers $30,000, but Is Refused. AMERICA IN LOAD TESTS Commander Nearly Ready as the Weather Still Holds Fliers -- Lindbergh Visits About City. BYRD'S PLANE, AMERICA, IN THE AIR AT ROOSEVELT FIELD YESTERDAY. BERTAUD MAY LOSE POST IN BELLANCA
stories.summary: Charles Levine considers replacing pilot Bertaud with Chamberlin for a potential record-breaking flight, as Commander Byrd's America plane undergoes load tests, and Lindbergh visits the city, during a time of intense aviation competition

stories.headline: FATE OF ATLANTIC FLIERS IS UNKNOWN AFTER DAY OF WATCHING AND RUMORS; BELLANCA PLANS TO START TOMORROW; WHITE BIRD MUST BE DOWN Grave Anxiety Is Felt for Nungesser and Coli, Now Long Overdue. WEATHER BAD ON ROUTE But Airmen Here Hope They May Be Safe -- Many Reports of Them Prove False. PARIS FEARS THE WORST Realizing Failure After Early News of Success, It Prays Fliers May Be Rescued. Map of Nungesser's Route Across Atlantic and Prevailing Weather Conditions
stories.summary: The fate of French aviators Nungesser and Coli remains unknown after their transatlantic flight, sparking anxiety and rumors, as the Bellanca plane prepares to start its own journey, amidst bad weather conditions

stories.headline: BULK OF I.R.T. STOCK IS HELD IN WALL ST., HEARING DISCLOSES; About 90 Per Cent. of It Is a "Stock Market Football," Untermyer Charges. DIRECTORS' HOLDINGS SMALL Hayden Stone, of Which Dahl Was Partner, Leads List of Voting Certificate Owners. MILLER WANTS 'FAIR VALUE' But B.M.T. Won't Insist on Replacement Cost in Case of Recapture, He Says at Opening of Inquiry. BULK OF I.R.T. STOCK IS HELD IN WALL ST.
stories.summary: A hearing reveals that about 90 percent of I.R.T. stock is held on Wall Street, with Hayden Stone leading the list of voting certificate owners, prompting concerns about the stock's value and ownership

stories.headline: COMMONS APPROVES BREAK WITH SOVIET; REJECTS LABOR PLEA; Motion for Inquiry Fails by Vote of 367 to 118, Order for Rupture Being Substituted. CLYNES ASKS 'FAIR TRIAL' Chamberlain Replies Verdict Is Already Justified -- Lloyd George Questions Result. WHITE PAPER OFFERS PROOF Government's Record of Moscow Intrigues Discloses Reds Here and Orders Sent From London. COMMONS APPROVES BREAK WITH SOVIET
stories.summary: The British Commons approves a break with the Soviet Union, rejecting a labor plea for an inquiry, with the government citing evidence of Moscow's intriques and justifying the rupture of diplomatic relations

rounds.blurb: Charles Lindbergh's transatlantic flight preparations progressed as Commander Byrd's America plane underwent load tests. The fate of French aviators Nungesser and Coli remained unknown, sparking anxiety. Britain's Commons approved a break with the Soviet Union, citing evidence of Moscow's intriques.
